fuzzy_prd: We need a general-purpose HTTP client utilities library for our service integrations. Developers should be able to build HTTP requests by providing a verb (get, post, put, delete), a URL, optional query parameters, headers, and a body payload. Query parameter names and values with special characters need to be percent-encoded automatically. When a URL has no scheme or host, the client should recognize it as relative and apply a placeholder internally. Payloads can be raw text, a JSON object, or a set of form fields — each should set the right content-type automatically, and if the content type doesn't already specify a charset, UTF-8 should be appended. We also need a small immutable key/value pair object, a parameter-map utility that can either drop or keep a listed set of keys without mutating the original, and some basic guard functions that reject null values, empty sequences, or false conditions with uniform error messages. There's also a need to serialize a few specific data records — timing pairs, confidence scores, and date lists — into compact positional arrays, following the same layout convention the audio-processing module uses for wire data. Finally, we need a way to resolve service credentials from a deployment configuration map: given a service name and optionally a plan name, find the right instance and return a standard authorization token.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public RequestBuilder method(HttpMethod method, String url)

trigger_keywords: what happens when URL is null
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A null URL is rejected with 'error=null_url'. A form fields list with an odd number of elements (cannot pair name/value) is rejected with 'error=odd_argument_count'.

trigger_keywords: how is charset added to content type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a raw string body has a media type that carries no charset, '; charset=utf-8' is appended. A JSON body always gets 'application/json; charset=utf-8'. A form body uses 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' (no charset appended).

trigger_keywords: how is a relative URL handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A path-only URL (no scheme/host) is treated as relative. A placeholder endpoint is applied internally so the request is well-formed. The 'relative' facet reports 'true'. The caller's path is retained exactly.

trigger_keywords: what does null name/value pair return
absent value sentinel in name-value pair
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For name/value pairs: creating with null name emits 'error=null_name'. An absent (null) value reports as '(null)' in output. The string form with present value is 'name=value'; with absent value is just 'name'.

trigger_keywords: how are param filter results ordered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: param_filter output entries are ordered by key for determinism, one 'key=value' per line. When the input map is null, the output is the single line 'null'. Keys requested for removal/selection that are not present are silently ignored.

trigger_keywords: credential resolution when service name is empty
which instance is selected without plan
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When service name is absent or empty, output is '(null)'. When plan is given, the instance with that plan is selected. When no plan, the first matching instance is selected. Token format: 'Basic ' + base64(username + ':' + password).
Word-timing: compact JSON array [word, start_time, end_time]. Word-confidence: [word, confidence]. Date list: compact JSON array preserving order, absent/null elements rendered as JSON null marker.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: following the same layout convention the audio-processing module uses for wire data
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 serialization — positional compact JSON arrays used by SpeechToText word timing (SpeechTimestamp) and word confidence (SpeechWordConfidence) models in the watson SDK

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the start.md specification and the golden reference implementation for details.

feature1_1_method_and_url.json.description: Construct an HTTP request for a given verb (GET, POST, PUT, or DELETE) and a target URL, then report the resulting request's HTTP method and its final URL. The URL passed in may already contain a query string, which must be preserved verbatim in the built request. This verifies the verb is recorded correctly and that a fully-qualified URL passes through unchanged.

feature1_2_query_params.json.description: Attach query parameters to a request whose base URL has no query string, then report the final URL. Each parameter is given as a name paired with either a single value or a list of values. A single value is appended once; a list value is expanded so the same parameter name is repeated for every element, preserving order. Parameter names and values that contain reserved or non-ASCII characters must be percent-encoded in the resulting URL.

feature1_3_relative_url.json.description: Build a request from a path-only URL (one that has no scheme or host, e.g. a leading-slash path). Such a request is treated as relative: a placeholder endpoint is applied internally so the request is well-formed, and the request can be recognized as relative. Report the HTTP method, whether the request is relative, and the path component of the request. The path supplied by the caller must be retained exactly.

feature1_4_headers.json.description: Attach one or more custom request headers (each a name and a value) while building a request, then report the HTTP method and the value stored under a chosen header name. Confirms that arbitrary headers supplied by the caller are recorded on the request and can be read back by name.

feature1_5_body.json.description: Attach a request payload of one of three kinds and report the serialized body together with its resolved content type. A raw string body paired with an explicit media type is sent as-is, and when the media type carries no charset a UTF-8 charset is appended to the content type. A JSON-object body is serialized to its compact JSON text and sent as JSON (charset UTF-8 appended). A set of form fields (an even-length flat list of name, value, name, value, ...) is encoded as an application/x-www-form-urlencoded body joining each pair with '=' and separating pairs with '&'.

feature1_6_invalid_usage.json.description: Reject malformed request-construction requests with a neutral, categorized error. Supplying no URL (a null URL) is rejected as a null-URL error. Supplying form fields as an odd-length list (so the name/value pairing is incomplete) is rejected as an odd-argument-count error. Each failure is reported as a single normalized error line and produces no request.

feature2_1_name_value_accessors.json.description: Create a name/value pair from a name and an optional value, then report the stored name, the stored value, and the pair's string form. The value may be absent (null). The string form is name followed by '=' followed by value when a value is present; when the value is absent the string form is just the name, and the reported value is shown as a null marker.

feature2_2_name_value_equality.json.description: Compare two name/value pairs for equality and report both whether they are considered equal and whether their hash codes match. Two pairs are equal exactly when both their names and their values are equal (with absent values treated consistently). Equal pairs must also share the same hash code. Pairs differing only in their value are not equal, and a pair with an absent value is not equal to one with the same name but a present value.

feature2_3_name_value_required.json.description: Require a non-null name when creating a name/value pair. Attempting to create a pair with a null name is rejected with a neutral, categorized error and produces no pair.

feature3_1_param_omit.json.description: Return a copy of a string-keyed map with the listed keys removed, leaving the original untouched. Keys requested for removal that are not present are ignored. When no keys are requested the copy is identical to the input. When the input map itself is absent (null), the result is also absent. The output lists each surviving key and value, one per line, ordered by key for determinism.

feature3_2_param_pick.json.description: Return a copy of a string-keyed map keeping only the listed keys, leaving the original untouched. Keys requested for selection that are not present are ignored. When no keys are requested the copy keeps every entry of the input. When the input map itself is absent (null), the result is also absent. The output lists each kept key and value, one per line, ordered by key for determinism; when no entries are kept the output is empty.

feature4_validation.json.description: Guard clauses that reject invalid arguments. A truth check fails when its condition is false. A presence check fails when the value is absent (null). A non-emptiness check fails when the sequence or text is empty. Each failure is reported as a neutral, categorized error naming which kind of check was violated; no other output is produced.

feature5_1_timestamp_serialization.json.description: Serialize a word-timing record to a compact JSON array of exactly three elements in fixed order: the word, its start time, and its end time. Times are emitted as numbers.

feature5_2_word_confidence_serialization.description: Serialize a word-confidence record to a compact JSON array of exactly two elements in fixed order: the word and its confidence. The confidence is emitted as a number.

feature5_3_epoch_date_serialization.json.description: Serialize a list of timestamps where each element is either an absent value or an epoch-milliseconds integer. The result is a compact JSON array preserving order, with absent elements rendered as a null marker.

feature6_credential_resolution.json.description: Resolve a service credential from a service-directory configuration. Given a service name and an optional plan, locate the matching service instance and return its credential token (HTTP basic authentication built from the instance's username and password). When the requested service name is absent or empty, no credential is returned (an absent marker is produced). When a plan is given, the instance with that plan is selected; when no plan is given, the first matching instance is selected.
